yeslyf · flow

House of Alpha · conscious money platform

yeslyfSay Yes to Life!

The complete app journey, one step at a time — every screen, what it does, the data it captures, the APIs it calls, and what it gives back. Follows the canonical 29-step master flow (New User Flow, 2026-06-03 revision) as the source of truth, layered with detail from the Figma prototype and updated per the three 2026-06-30 calls — the 11:30 internal review, the 3 PM review with Bhuvanaa & Harish, and the developer-agency call — plus the 02-Jul external-API research pack (External API/).

29master-flow steps
4+1tiers
8integrations
8containers
5engine outputs
~6mto first insight

How to read this

A conversational journey, decoded

The app feels like a chat with the brand. Each step below pairs the real screen (or a placeholder where none exists yet) with its spec: what it does, what you do, the external APIs it calls and the internal systems it uses (tap a system for full detail), the input captured and the output produced. Tier tags use the product's own colours.

Free Tier 1 · Financial Plan Tier 2 · Investment Plan Tier 3 · Subscription Tier 4 · Human Advisory Engine output → saved to dock ✶ External API · third-party call Our System · internal engine (tap to open) Spec-sourced · no Figma yet

First — the money ladder

One funnel, four paid steps

This is Yeslyf's money ladder. Get people in for free, then sell progressively more valuable plans. Most users move down the list over time (Free → 1 → 2 → 3); you can skip steps, but Tier 2 is gated on real portfolio data + a risk profile — not on having bought the tier before it. Prototype pricing: Essential ₹2,999 · Growth ₹4,999. The tier badges on every step below refer to these.

Tier access — who sees each screen

The Excel spec gates screens with a 3-option purchase model — Option 1 / 2 / 3 = Tier 1 / 2 / 3 (Financial Plan / Investment Plan / Full-Service Subscription). Tier 4 · Human Advisory is the off-app layer that Full Service (Option 3) feeds into. The access tag on each step below reads as:

  • Free  free tier only
  • Tier 1 Tier 2 Tier 3  that specific paid tier (Excel “Option 1 / 2 / 3”)
  • Tier 1+ Tier 2+  that tier and every tier above it — so Tier 1+ = T1·T2·T3, Tier 2+ = T2·T3 (Excel “Option 1+ / 2+”)
  • All  every tier, Free included
  • Free Tier 1  the locked-teaser case (Excel “Free + Opt 1”) — Free & Tier-1 see a teaser, Tier 2+ get the full feature (e.g. the Hell Yes teaser, card 26h)

The flow

Step by step, splash to advisor

Grouped into six journey phases plus a final set of global / system states (Flow G — splash, errors, auto-save, session resume, purchase & booking flows). Each step shows what it does, what the user does, the External API it calls vs the Our System engines it uses (tap a system chip for its full inputs, outputs, calculations and why), the data captured (and how it's stored), and what it produces. The tier badges refer to the ladder above.

Before the app — the YesLifers Pledge. The first three cards (P1–P3) are pre-app / community screens that sit ahead of the canonical 29-step master flow: the pledge happens in the YesLifers community (a separate Supabase app). Cold-funnel users take it as an on-ramp; warm-funnel users arrive with pledge_taken already set. The numbered Steps 1–29 follow the New User Flow 03-06-2026.xlsx (S4) master flow exactly; L1–L3 are the post-delivery living-app layer, not yet folded into S4's numbering. The 8 commitments' wording isn't in any source file.
Cold-funnel vs warm-funnel — the two paths into the app
❄ Cold funnel — brand new users
Entry
Marketing / SEO / referral → landing page → sign-up + OTP
Pledge
P1–P3 shown — 8 commitments, each individually tapped. Mandatory for all cold-funnel users — sets pledge_taken = true.
Identity
New AWS Cognito account created via email login (per S4 Step 2) — see Open conflict re: email vs Google/Apple OAuth
Avatar
Created during onboarding
Community
Optional — sets a separate community_joined flag. User may pledge without joining; can join later. Required before paywall — both pledge_taken and community_joined must be true to become a paid user.
Screens
Full path — P1/P2/P3 + all 29 numbered steps (Step 1–29)
☀ Warm funnel — arriving from YesLifers community
Entry
Founder nudge / event CTA / cohort / "I want a plan" thread
Pledge
P1–P3 skipped — pledge_taken already true in Supabase. community_joined is also already true — both flags satisfied, ready for paywall.
Identity
Inherited — email confirmed, display name + avatar carried over
Avatar
Skipped if already set in the community
Community
On-ramp skipped — already a YesLifer, community_joined = true inherited
Screens
≈4–7 fewer screens — drops directly into Step 4 (Q1+Q2+Q3)
✦ Meeting 2026-06-30 — Two flags, not one: The call clarified that pledge_taken and community_joined are separate boolean flags. Every user (cold or warm) must have pledge_taken = true to enter the app. Joining the YesLifers community is optional pre-paywall. However, to become a paid user, both flags must be true — a user who pledged but never joined the community cannot pay. Pledge screen opens in an in-app browser; user can close and return later. The paywall gate must check community_joined and surface a join CTA if it is false.

Flow map — where each screen sits

✦ Requested at the 2026-06-30 call (“get a nice flow diagram in terms of the steps… follow along where I am”). Tap a pill to jump to that step; the pill you are reading highlights as you scroll.

Superseded reference · S7 Boardmix

Stage 0–6 master flow (historical)

This Boardmix stage-skeleton predates and is now superseded by S4 (New User Flow 03-06-2026.xlsx), which this document treats as canonical for step numbering throughout. Boardmix introduced ideas — income capture up-front (Stage 1.2), a 3-entry-path fork (Stage 1.3), the Mirror Moment identification gate, a 3-layer nudge system, and the 5-dimension living dashboard — that did not make it into S4's final 1–29 list. Retained here for historical context and because some of its ideas (the nudge system, the living dashboard) may still be worth reviving for the post-delivery living-app layer (L1–L3). Source: Boardmix screenshot 2026-06-26 [S7].

StageStepScreenKey elementsUser actionNotes / conflicts
0–Start ScreenLogo, CTA buttonTap to beginEntry point
11Splash ScreenBrand logo, animation, auto-advanceWait⚠ "Wait 5 seconds" here vs "≤2s, no spinner" in S2 → G01. Direct conflict.
12Awareness QuestionsQ1: Income / month — income band captured before persona matching. Likely seeds the Quick Report.Select income band+ New here. Not in S2, S4, or the Figma prototype. Stage 1.2.
13Profile Options3 entry paths: "Show me my full profile" · "Connect AA" · "I'll answer questions"Choose path+ AA-vs-manual fork at the very start. S2/S4 restrict AA to post-paywall Tier 2+. ⚠ Conflict.
2A1Life Stage QuestionQ1, options A–DSelectStored as L1 (session only)
2A2Emotional State QuestionQ2, options A–DSelectStored as E1 (session only)
2A3Aspiration QuestionQ3, options A–DSelectStored as G1 (session only)
2A4Quick ReportGenerated rough profile summary + disclaimer + "See in-depth analysis" CTA. Sits between Q3 and the Mirror Moment.View / proceed+ New here. Not in S2/S4/Figma.
2B1The Mirror MomentProfile Card (1 of 6) + Identification Gate: Yes / Only some / Not reallyChoose resonance+ Amendment 3. Formalises the "show me a different profile" rule (S3 §5.4) into an explicit gated screen.
2B2Profile Matching6 profile variants displaySelect best matchProfile-specific badge. ⚠ S3 says the matcher picks one server-side — clarify whether the user actively picks from 6 or is shown one.
2B3Amendment FallbackAll-three-rejected fallback — retry or skipRetry or skip+ Hesitation detection. Implied in S3 rules; made explicit here.
31Section 1 — Income & CashflowQ1.1–Q1.4, Q2.1–Q2.3AnswerNew 4-section taxonomy vs S2's B/C/D split. ⚠ Q1.1–Q4.5 numbering doesn't map 1:1 to S2's I1/YB/YL field codes — crosswalk needed before build.
32Section 2 — Investments & WealthQ3.1–Q3.3, Q4.1Answer
33Section 3 — Protection & LiabilitiesQ4.2a–Q4.3Answer
34Section 4 — Goals & TimelineQ4.4–Q4.5Answer
35AA ConsentConsent request for account aggregationAccept / declineConditional. ⚠ Offered mid-flow here vs post-paywall only in S2/S4.
36Loading ScreenRotating content carouselWaitStage 3 closing
41Carousel Reveal4-slide carouselSwipe throughMoment transitions
42The DiagnosticCurrent financial state analysisView insightsMoment 1 — maps to Clarity insights (B12–B14)
43The Blind SpotHidden financial gaps revealedView insightsMoment 2
44What Could BePotential outcomes visual card — anchored to Q3 aspirationView cardMoment 3. Maps to S3 §5.4 rule 5 (aspiration anchoring): A=freedom/time · B=safety/legacy · C=permission/enjoyment · D=confidence.
45Upgrade PromptOptions 1, 2, 3, 4 + "Book call" CTA — all four tiers visible at the paywallSelect tier or book+ Advisor booking inline at paywall. Confirms all 4 tiers surface here.
51Post-Plan UpgradeOption 1 → 2 → 3 upgrade pathsUpgrade selectionTrigger-based
52Post-Plan Mirror MomentRe-reflection flow — reassess persona after the plan is deliveredRe-assess profile+ Amendment 6. Entirely absent from S2/S4/Figma.
61Home ScreenDashboard by tier, home-screen contentNavigateReturning user — maps to F01–F04
62Living Dashboard5 tracking dimensions with Chart Placement Map: WAR · Goals progress · Protection score · Cashflow health · Risk alignmentView / interact+ 5-dimension dashboard. More specific than S2's Flow-F homes. Real-time updates via AA.
63Action Completion"How It Works" section, task trackingComplete actionsMaps to Yes Pathway checklist (F05)
64Nudge System3-layer nudge system: Layer 1 soft (micro-copy in flow) · Layer 2 medium (projection teasers, dock items) · Layer 3 hard (direct upgrade CTA)Engage / dismiss+ 3-layer nudges. Not in S2/S4.
What Boardmix adds vs Figma + Excel
Stage 1.2 — Income up-front: an income band is captured before persona matching — likely the seed for the Quick Report (2A.4). Not in S2, S4, or the Figma prototype.
Stage 1.3 — 3-entry-path fork: AA connect / full-profile / questions-only — the AA-vs-manual decision surfaced at the very start, not mid-flow or post-paywall.
Mirror Moment + Identification Gate (2B.1): Yes / Only some / Not really — formalises the "show me a different profile" rule from S3 into an explicit gated screen with an Amendment Fallback.
3-layer nudge system (Stage 6.4): Soft → Medium → Hard. Layer 1: in-flow micro-copy. Layer 2: projection teasers + dock items. Layer 3: direct upgrade CTA.
5-dimension living dashboard (Stage 6.2): WAR · Goals progress · Protection score · Cashflow health · Risk alignment. More granular than S2's F01–F04 tier homes.
⚠ Boardmix conflicts to resolve before build: splash 5s vs ≤2s · income up-front vs after goals · AA as entry-path vs post-paywall-only · Q1.1–Q4.5 numbering has no 1:1 map to S2's I1/YB/YL/INV field codes (crosswalk needed) · persona display (user picks from 6 vs server picks 1). A single reconciled master flow does not yet exist.

§6C · NWGC engine workbook parsed sheet-by-sheet

What's inside each PDF deliverable

Three deliverables ship across Steps 24/28/29 — Financial Plan (Tier 1), Investment Plan (Tier 2), Action Plan (all tiers from T1). They are assembled from the NWGC engine's output blocks. Source: SG & HS NWGC OCT 2025.xlsm [S6]. The engine produces a far richer output than what the Figma currently surfaces as inline cards — gaps flagged at the bottom.

Output block Key user-facing fields App screen Financial Plan
Tier 1 · ₹2,999		 Investment Plan
Tier 2 · ₹4,999		 Action Plan
all tiers from T1
Net Worth Total realisable, unrealisable, assets, liabilities, net worth per owner + total Step 14 · B12 ✓✓—
Weighted Avg Return (WAR) Per-asset weight %, ERR, WAR contribution; portfolio WAR % (sample ≈7.57%); equity/debt/RE sub-totals Step 14 · B12 ✓✓—
Projections / Accumulation Year-by-year (age→100): net worth, returns, investments, goals, expenses, loans outstanding; current path vs better allocation vs full potential B13 / D08 / Step 22 ✓✓—
Budget / Cashflow Income (nominal + inflation-adj), 11 expense categories, surplus/deficit per year, current vs retirement lifestyle D06 / Step 22 ✓——
Life Events timeline Inflation-adjusted cost per event, down-payment, loan value/term, withdrawal year C06 / Step 18 ✓——
Goal / Scenario (NWGC AS-IS vs SCENARIO) Two-path projection across ages: net worth, income, expenses, life events, investments; "money runs out at age" L2 · Hell Yes E ✓——
Protection — HLV (life-cover gap) Existing cover, liabilities, expense ratio → additional insurance required (sample ₹2.92 Cr) B09 · Step 21 ✓——
Protection — Health Insurance Need Risk score from age / location / health & family history / lifestyle → Final Score + Category; recommended Base + Top-up + Critical Illness (1× annual expenses) + Accidental (2× annual income) B09 · Step 21 ✓——
Wheel of Wealth Radar scored 0–10 across 5 dimensions: Risk Cover · Cashflow · Asset Growth · Will-the-money-last · Succession Readiness F04 health score ✓——
Loan Amortization Per-loan: balance, EMI, interest, principal, rate, term, payoff date B08 · Step 13 ✓——
Allocation gap & action report Current vs target allocation; gap analysis; buy / sell / hold per holding; risk-adjusted suitability per holding F06 · Step 28 —✓—
Yes Pathway checklist Prioritised actions — This Week · This Month · Month 5+. Sourced from gap analysis + most urgent engine outputs F05 · Step 29 ✓✓✓ primary
Why does the Action Plan column only have one tick? The Action Plan is not a condensed version of the FP — it is a single, lean deliverable: the Yes Pathway checklist (screens F05 / Step 29). It contains only prioritised actions — This Week · This Month · Month 5+ — drawn from the most urgent items in the gap analysis. The 11 rows above it describe content that lives exclusively inside the FP and IP PDFs. The Action Plan ships to every paid tier from T1 onwards.
Design gap — engine outputs not yet surfaced as Figma screens

The current Figma prototype surfaces only Net Worth, WAR, Projections, and Surplus as inline chat cards (Steps 13/18). The engine already computes HLV insurance gap, health-cover recommendation, Wheel-of-Wealth score, and the full year-by-year scenario tables — these belong in the FP PDF but have no Figma screen yet. Decision needed: show these inline in the chat, or PDF-only?

New · Investment Schema — Harish, 3 PM call 2026-06-30

The Tier-2 investment engine — factors, archetypes, allocations

The recommendation framework behind the Investment Plan (Steps 25–29) and the living rebalancing loop, presented by Harish and captured from Investment Schema.docx. Harish supplies input/output specs, not algorithm code; Vatsal defines the output formats so Ishtiyak can design the screens and the data model can be built. No AI anywhere in this pipeline — every input→output is mathematical, deterministic and auditable (SEBI/RBI comfort).

Basic inputs → five internal factors

Inputs (all already captured in the flow): age · monthly income · monthly committed expenses · investment horizon (Short <3y / Medium 3–7y / Long 7y+) · self-declared risk comfort · goals / life events.

FactorWhat it encodes
SIR — Surplus Income RatioSavings as % of income (existing wealth folds in) — how aggressive the model can go.
LPS — Liquidity Preference ScoreHow much must stay liquid; function of age, emergency funds (a 62-year-old ≫ a 25-year-old).
THC — Time Horizon ClusterShort / medium / long, derived from goals & life events — when the money is needed.
RDI — Risk Disposition IndexStated risk appetite adjusted down by the data — low surplus or a 2-year horizon lowers the score regardless of what the user claims.
TES — Tax Efficiency ScoreOptional for v1 — Harish leans toward including it for a mass-retail audience (‘what about tax?’ is the first question people ask). May end up a flag on other archetypes rather than its own.

Six archetypes · base allocation ranges (RDI tilts within the range)

ArchetypeProfilePrimary needEquityDebtAlt/GoldLiquid
The AccumulatorHigh SIR, Low LPS, Long THCAggressive wealth building70–80%10–20%5–8%2–5%
The BalancerModerate SIR/LPS, Mixed THCBalanced growth + safety45–55%25–35%8–12%8–12%
The ProtectorAny SIR, High LPS, Short THCCapital preservation + liquidity20–30%35–45%3–7%25–35%
The Tax OptimizerHigh TES, high bracketTax-efficient growth (NPS, ELSS, debt)45–55%25–35%3–7%10–15%
The Life EventerSpecific 2–5-yr goal (home, child)Goal-specific earmarking35–45%30–40%3–7%15–25%
The Course CorrectorChaotic existing portfolioRestructuring + rationalisation40–50%25–35%8–12%10–15%

Harish expects more archetypes (Gaurav: make as many as needed so nothing gets tweaked ad-hoc later); the Tax Optimizer may dissolve into a flag. The scoring algorithm behind the factors is not ready — broad logic only.

Curated product shelf — tagged on five axes

Axes: asset class · liquidity (T+0 → lock-in) · tax treatment · min investment · risk band (1–5). Within a bucket the product split is standardised, not per-client (e.g. within 35% liquid: first 10% → product X, next 10% → product Y).

Equity — growth

Large-cap index funds · flexi-cap active MFs · ELSS (tax-linked) · mid-cap index (high RDI)

Debt — stable

PPF (long, tax-free) · NPS Tier I (retirement) · corporate bond funds · short-duration debt MFs

Liquid — buffer

Liquid MFs · overnight funds · arbitrage funds · penalty-free FDs

Alternatives — hedge

Gold ETF / SGB · REITs (HNI-adjacent) · international MF (USD hedge)

Goal-specific

Sukanya Samriddhi (girl child) · capital-protection FoFs · target-date hybrid funds

Rebalancing engine — exactly two triggers

TriggerMechanics
1 · Client-level driftCash-flow calendar (time-to-goal shrinking → shift allocation) + time-based strategic rebalance (half-yearly/annual) with automatic profit-booking on drift (equity 50→60 after a rally → book).
2 · Market-regime overlayNifty PE/PB vs 15-yr historical percentile (90th pct = expensive → reduce equity tilt) · India VIX level (defensive vs neutral tilt) · a 3-state macro flag (expansion / neutral / contraction). Factors not final. ⚠ Research note: Nifty P/E has a structural break (Apr-2021 standalone→consolidated switch) — lead with the P/B percentile; index/VIX display needs an NSE data license (External API/11).

Exact outputs: model portfolio allocation with product break-up → existing portfolio (via AA / NSDL CAS — deliberately not an input to the model portfolio) → action plan: what to redeem, what to reinvest, delivered as tasks + nudges. Instruments can be earmarked to goals (the ‘car in 6 months’ example) so users don’t redeem them.

Open: number of archetypes · factor scoring algorithm · product-level segregation for subjective holdings (e.g. already holds direct equity) · TES in/out · market-regime factor selection. Harish shares the framework with the team for holes; Vatsal to define output formats + brief Ishtiyak.

§5 · Profile Matching Cards.docx

The six persona cards

The matcher (Step 4) selects one of six pre-rendered profiles from Q1 + Q2 + Q3 in <100ms. Each card is defined by a dominant financial-flaw archetype — the hidden pattern holding the user back. All six cards are cached at app init; the matcher returns a profile ID only. Source: Profile Matching Cards.docx [S3].

Rajesh
Self-Neglect · default fallback
Established, responsible, caring for everyone around him — and deprioritising himself. High earner, no structured plan of his own. "I'm paying for everything — but am I actually building anything for myself?" Sample card: 41 · Pune · Senior Manager · ₹2.1L/month. Before/after: ₹1.67 Cr (money runs out age 68) → ₹7.79 Cr (age 94).
Flaw: Always puts family needs ahead of wealth-building. Money moves, nothing compounds toward his own future.
Q1Earning well but uncertain
Q2Confident but uncertain underneath or Anxious — something feels off
Q3Security for family or Not sure yet — clarity first
Default fallback when no profile scores ≥5. "Earning well but uncertain" Q1 is the primary signal — if someone earns well but can't explain where it goes, they almost always land here.
Arjun & Meera
Overconfidence
High-earning couple — dual income, building their foundation — with a FIRE plan built on optimistic, un-stress-tested assumptions. They know they're on track. That's the problem.
Flaw: Plans that feel solid haven't been tested against real life events, inflation, or actual goal costs.
Q1Building my foundation
Q2Confident but uncertain underneath
Q3Financial independence or Freedom to make big choices
All three answers must align for a strong match. The "confident-but-uncertain" Q2 is what separates them from Nisha — same life stage, but Arjun & Meera feel ahead while Nisha feels lost.
Nisha
Directionlessness
High earner investing without structure or goal-mapping. Money goes in — somewhere — but there's no picture of where it's going or why. "No clear picture yet."
Flaw: Activity (investing) without direction (goals). Busy doing the right things for the wrong reasons.
Q1Building my foundation
Q2No clear picture yet
Q3Financial independence or Not sure yet — clarity first
"No clear picture yet" Q2 is the decisive split from Arjun & Meera — same Q1 life stage, completely different emotional state. Activity (investing) without direction (goals).
Vikram & Priya
Inattention
Young family, dual income, growing-family complexity. Reactive financial decisions — responding to life events as they come, no unified plan. Too busy to manage money.
Flaw: Good intentions, no execution. Finances keep getting pushed. EMI-heavy and under-protected.
Q1Growing family complexity
Q2Busy — finances keep getting pushed or Anxious or No clear picture yet
Q3Security for family or Not sure yet — clarity first
"Growing family" Q1 is the primary signal — the life stage dominates. Edge case: Q1=B + Q2=Busy + Q3=Security ties with Rajesh in the raw scoring; Vikram & Priya wins that tiebreaker. ⚠ Open implementation decision.
Aditya
Conflation · most specific profile
Business owner in a transition — personal and business finances blurred. Can't cleanly separate personal net worth from business cash flows, loans, and assets.
Flaw: Personal wealth is entangled with the business — planning is impossible without separation.
Q1In a transition
Q2Confident but uncertain underneath or Busy — finances keep getting pushed
Q3Financial independence — options not obligations
"Financial independence" Q3 is what separates Aditya from Deepika & Sanjay when both answer "in a transition." If Q1=D but Q3≠Financial independence and Q2 isn't Confident/Busy, defaults to Deepika & Sanjay. Confirm with: "Are you self-employed?"
Deepika & Sanjay
Outdated Assumptions
Income transition — one income now irregular, or a dual-income plan under strain. Life has changed; the financial plan hasn't caught up. Mid-career anxiety meets mid-life reality.
Flaw: The plan was built for a life that no longer exists. Assumptions (income, expenses, goals) need a full reset.
Q1In a transition
Q2Anxious — something feels off
Q3Security for family or Not sure yet — clarity first
"Anxious" Q2 is the decisive split when Q1=D — Deepika & Sanjay feel the fear, Aditya is still in control. Both share a single scoring column in the raw tables; the tiebreaker rules must be coded explicitly.
Edge cases & override rules [S3 → §5.4]
  • Default fallback → Rajesh if no profile scores ≥5. Most universally relatable; lowest misID risk.
  • "Show me a different profile" → Show next-highest scorer; never repeat; max 3 profiles per session. Store the full ranked score array at match time — don't recalculate.
  • Anxious-tone override → If Q2=C (Anxious), apply the gentle tone variant through Stages 3–4; deliver the blind-spot insight "with extra care."
  • Aspiration anchoring → Q3 is stored and passed to Stage 4's "What Could Be" moment: A = foreground freedom/time · B = safety/legacy · C = permission/enjoyment · D = confidence/peace-of-mind.
  • B·B·B tie-breaker: Q1=B + Q2=B + Q3=B maps to both Rajesh (★★ Strong) and Vikram & Priya (★★★ Primary). ⚠ Decide which wins before build — this is open decision #9 (see below).
Onboarding question reference

Click the Q1 / Q2 / Q3 badges on any persona card to jump to that question below.

Q1 — Life stage · stored as L1

"Where are you in life right now?"

A Building my foundation
B Growing family complexity
C Earning well but uncertain
D In a transition

Q2 — Emotional state · stored as E1

"How does your financial life feel right now?"

A Confident but uncertain underneath
B Busy — finances keep getting pushed
C Anxious — something feels off
D No clear picture yet

Q3 — Aspiration · stored as G1

"What would feel like success five years from now?"

A Financial independence — options not obligations
B Security for my family
C Freedom to make big choices
D Not sure yet — I need clarity first

Before anyone builds

Resolved, and still open

✦ Updated from team call — 2026-06-30  ·  Gaurav, Vatsal, Kajal & Somil. Six new decisions resolved; three new open actions added. Items needing Bhuvna / Ishtiyak input are marked with their name.

Three flow specs exist (Figma chat, Excel screens, Boardmix stages) and they disagree on several points. Items marked ✦ Meeting 2026-06-30 were resolved on the team call (Vatsal, Gaurav, Kajal, Somil). Open items with an Action label are waiting on Bhuvna’s or Ishtiyak’s input before build. The remaining conflicts still need a human call.

§11.2 · Before the partner gets a quote

10 open decisions that affect the build

These are genuine blockers — unresolved client decisions that each change the scope or architecture of what the partner builds. A quote written before these are answered will be wrong. Source: S1 → Doc 00 Open Decisions.

Decision 1

Account Aggregator vendor

Setu / Anumati / OneMoney / CAMSFinServ / Finvu — pricing varies 3–5× across vendors. Integration effort, consent-handle behaviour, and FIP coverage differ materially. Pick one for v1. ✦ Research verdict (02-Jul): Setu — self-serve sandbox with mock FIPs, absorbs the ReBIT signing/crypto (≈3–4× less effort); ~₹10–25/fetch unofficial, get a quote. Prereq either way: Sahamati FIU certification for HoA. See External API/01_Account_Aggregator.md.

Affects: AA integration estimate
Decision 2

KYC vendor

Digio vs IDfy vs Hyperverge. Turnaround SLA, failure rate, DigiLocker integration depth, and fallback strategy differ. This decision drives the post-paywall KYC flow design (Step 25). ✦ Research verdict (02-Jul): Digio primary — the only vendor bundling KYC + Aadhaar eSign + eStamp + KRA in one contract; IDfy as penny-drop/PAN failover. ≈₹30–70 all-in per completed Tier-2 user. See External API/02_KYC_eSign.md.

Affects: KYC flow + cost
Decision 3

WhatsApp BSP

AiSensy / Gupshup / Wati — all three named in the spec. The BSP choice affects message-template approval timelines, pricing per conversation, and analytics available. ✦ Research verdict (02-Jul): none of the three — go direct with the Meta Cloud API from Container 5 (zero markup, first-party webhooks; utility ≈₹0.13–0.15/msg). Gupshup only if a BSP is forced; Wati ruled out. Watch template reclassification (marketing = 7–8× cost). See External API/06_WhatsApp_BSP.md.

Affects: notification layer
Decision 4

API language — Python vs Node

FastAPI (Python) is recommended because it matches the engine's language, removing a language boundary at the Container 4 interface. Node is acceptable. Must decide before the backend architecture is committed.

Affects: backend estimate
Decision 5

KYC trigger tier — Tier 1 or Tier 2?

The current spec assumes KYC at Tier 2. But if the Financial Plan (Tier 1) constitutes "advice" under SEBI RIA, KYC may be required at Tier 1. Compliance must adjudicate before the paywall and onboarding flows are designed. ✦ 3 PM call + research: the trigger is personalised advice for a fee, not tier — data collection needs no agreement (Harish); if Tier 1 outputs any risk-profiled recommendation, KYC + the signed agreement land at Tier 1 too. To counsel (Kajal owns).

Affects: paywall + onboarding scope
Decision 6

Pricing — FP / IP / Subscription

FP = ₹2,999 (Essential) and IP = ₹4,999 (Growth) are confirmed by the Figma prototype. Tier-3 subscription price is still unconfirmed — must be set before the paywall and billing flows are finalised.

Affects: paywall design + billing
Decision 7

Subscription model — monthly only vs annual

Monthly Razorpay mandate is the current default. An annual option changes the billing-lifecycle code, dunning logic, refund policy, and the subscription-state machine in Chart 08.

Affects: billing lifecycle
Decision 8

Advisor Console — separate app vs inside yeslifers-web

Container 6 (Advisor Console) can be a standalone React app on the same AWS stack, or routed through the YesLifers Supabase property. Separate is cleaner for DPDP; routing through Supabase adds identity complexity.

Affects: Container 6 estimate
Decision 9

Tier-4 scheduling — Calendly vs Cal.com vs manual

Advisor booking (Step L3) needs a scheduling integration. Calendly is easiest. Cal.com is self-hosted and avoids a third-party dependency. Manual booking (support ops) defers all engineering but has no in-app confirmation. ✦ Research verdict (02-Jul): manual/Calendly for MVP → Cal.com Platform when booking goes native in-app (Calendly has no booking-creation API — webview forever). Self-hosting now means the Cal.diy fork (Cal.com went closed-source Apr-2026). See External API/13_Scheduling_Calendly_Calcom.md.

Affects: advisor booking flow
Decision 10

Refund window — 7 / 14 / 30 days

Governs all downgrade paths, the Admin Console refund surface (Surface 4), and user expectations at the paywall. No duration is set in any source document. Must be decided before the billing lifecycle and the support-ops runbook are designed.

Affects: billing + Admin Console
Decision 11 · new — dev-agency call 2026-06-30

Martech / CRM stack — and where app data lives

Opened on the developer-agency call: pick an omni-channel lead manager (SFMC vs NetCore discussed), define prospect-vs-lead handling incl. off-app leads (LinkedIn, newsletter), decide whether app data lands in the raw DB, a CRM or a CMS (avoid duplicate databases), and specify the CRM→app action set (nudges, notifications, emails, discounts). Daily workshops agreed — order: external APIs → admin panel → advisor panel → marketing.

Affects: admin console + architecture + notification layer

For the build partner · Doc 00 + Charts 05–08

The system underneath

The flow above is the user's view. This is the builder's view — the containers and stack that run it, the external partners it depends on, and the four system sequences (Charts 05–08) that each screen compresses into a single line. Design-level; the build partner owns the physical detail. ✦ 02-Jul research pack: every external integration now has a field-level research file — endpoints, I/O, auth, webhooks, pricing, India compliance — in External API/ (start at 00_INDEX.md). Wiring the brown API chips in the flow to these files is the next doc task the dev team asked for.

Critical scope split for the quote: the Financial Planning Engine (Container 4) is built and maintained by the internal HoA team — the partner builds only an API wrapper around it. This must be explicit in the contract, or engine work gets scoped into the partner's quote twice.
Eight containers · who builds what
External integrations · each a separate partner line-item
Stack & environments
System sequences · Charts 05–08 — the screens compress these to a line each

The data model

19 entities, household at the root

The complete logical schema from the architecture doc (Chart 09). Household is the root — most financial entities hang off it, not the user. Design-level only: physical types, indexes and migrations are the build partner's job. (PK) primary key · (FK) foreign key.

SEBI RIA + DPDP

The compliance spine

Every advice-bearing event is logged to an append-only trail; users get their DPDP rights without breaking SEBI's 5-year retention. From Charts 10 & 12.

DPDP data-subject rights — three flows the user can trigger
Internal Admin Console · Chart 13

Internal tooling · Chart 13

The admin console — 7 surfaces, 4 roles

Often dismissed as “just CRUD.” It isn’t — it’s the operational backbone, and the chart most likely to be under-quoted. Role-gated; every sensitive action is audited.

Scope note for the quote: honest v1 estimate is 4–6 engineering weeks (many agencies quote 1–2). Role-gating must be enforced at the API layer, not just hidden in the UI — deferring it is a compliance failure.
The four roles · who they are
Who can do what · role × surface access matrix

✓ full access · gated / limit / review / esc. partial or conditional · — no access. Admin/Engineer inherits every surface.

The seven surfaces · in detail
Platform & enforcement notes

Built around one idea: say yes, on purpose.
